---
title: "Utopías of Iztapalapa"
subtitle: "Who reaches the network, and what distinguishes the sites that draw from across the city"
author: "Ollin D. Langle-Chimal"
date: today
format:
  html:
    page-layout: full
    body-width: 100%
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    html-math-method: katex
jupyter: python3
---

## Setup

The analysis works from the published per-site table. Each row is one Utopía,
with its visitor counts, home-to-site distances, wealth mix, place-level
segregation, transit supply, service catalog and surrounding crime already
aggregated. The upstream steps that build these columns from the raw mobility,
transit and crime feeds are not part of this repository.

```{python}
import os
os.chdir("..")  # run from the repository root

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

from data import load_summary, load_items, build_axes, domain_counts
from stats import (correlation_table, standardized_regression, partial_axes,
                   correlate, permutation_pearson)
from figures import style, scatter_fit, barh_sites, corr_heatmap, typology_plot, label

style()

m = build_axes(load_summary())
items = load_items()
sites = list(m["utopia_name"])
m[["utopia_name", "n_unique_visitors", "median_distance_km", "median_wealth",
   "place_seg_S_a", "transit_score", "distinctiveness_index",
   "violent_share", "composite_score"]].round(3)
```

## Headline correlations

Every relationship is a Pearson correlation over the thirteen sites, with a
two-sided permutation p-value; visit counts enter on a log10 scale. With
$n = 13$ we read $p$ between 0.05 and 0.10 as suggestive rather than settled.

```{python}
pairs = [
    ("transit_score", "n_unique_visitors", "transit -> visitors", True),
    ("distinctiveness_index", "n_unique_visitors", "distinctiveness -> visitors", True),
    ("distinctiveness_index", "weekend_distance_premium_km", "distinctiveness -> weekend premium", False),
    ("violent_share", "mean_wealth", "violent share -> visitor wealth", False),
    ("violent_share", "mean_distance_km", "violent share -> distance", False),
    ("violent_share", "place_seg_S_a", "violent share -> segregation", False),
    ("crime_per_km2", "n_unique_visitors", "crime density -> visitors", True),
    ("place_seg_S_a", "mean_distance_km", "segregation -> distance", False),
    ("access_gravity", "n_unique_visitors", "gravity access -> visitors", True),
    ("composite_score", "n_unique_visitors", "composite -> visitors", True),
]
correlation_table(m, pairs)
```

Transit supply is the strongest correlate of how many people come; the
distinctiveness of the offer is unrelated to volume but tracks how much farther
weekend visitors travel; the violent share of surrounding crime tracks who comes
rather than how many.

## The three axes together

```{python}
partial_axes(m)
```

```{python}
standardized_regression(m)
```

Controlling for services and crime leaves the transit association intact, and it
carries essentially all of the composite's link to volume.

## Service offer and travel distance

```{python}
attrs = [("n_facilities", "Facilities"), ("n_landmark", "Landmark items"),
         ("avg_rarity", "Avg. rarity"), ("avg_infra", "Avg. infrastructure"),
         ("sum_distinctiveness", "Sum distinctiveness"),
         ("distinctiveness_index", "Distinctiveness index")]
outcomes = [("n_unique_visitors", "Visitors"), ("mean_distance_km", "Mean distance"),
            ("weekend_distance_premium_km", "Weekend premium"),
            ("weekend_share", "Weekend share"), ("recurrence_rate", "Recurrence"),
            ("mean_wealth", "Visitor wealth"), ("place_seg_S_a", "Segregation")]
corr_heatmap(m, attrs, outcomes, "heat_attributes", logy_cols={"n_unique_visitors"})
scatter_fit(m, "distinctiveness_index", "weekend_distance_premium_km",
            "Service distinctiveness index", "Weekend distance premium (km)",
            "distinct_vs_premium")
```

The weekend distance premium is the column that responds to the offer; the raw
visitor count does not.

```{python}
dom = domain_counts(items, sites)
dom_pred = [(c, c) for c in dom.columns]
md = m.set_index("utopia_name").join(dom)[[c for c in dom.columns]].reset_index()
md = md.rename(columns={"index": "utopia_name"}).merge(
    m[["utopia_name"] + [c for _, c in outcomes]], on="utopia_name")
corr_heatmap(md, dom_pred, outcomes, "heat_domains", logy_cols={"n_unique_visitors"})
```

## Transit and visitor volume

```{python}
tmetrics = [("transit_score", "Transit score"), ("n_routes_1km", "Routes"),
            ("n_modes_1km", "Modes"), ("weekday_trips_1km", "Weekday trips"),
            ("weekend_transit_ratio", "Weekend/weekday"),
            ("has_metro_1km", "Metro"), ("has_brt_1km", "BRT")]
corr_heatmap(m, tmetrics, outcomes, "heat_transit", logy_cols={"n_unique_visitors"})
scatter_fit(m, "transit_score", "n_unique_visitors", "Transit accessibility score",
            "Unique visitors", "axis_transit_vs_visitors", logy=True)
```

## Surrounding violence and who is reached

```{python}
scatter_fit(m, "violent_share", "mean_wealth", "Violent share of crime",
            "Mean visitor wealth", "violent_vs_wealth", statloc="upper right")
scatter_fit(m, "violent_share", "mean_distance_km", "Violent share of crime",
            "Mean distance from home (km)", "violent_vs_distance", statloc="upper right")
```

## Wealth, distance and segregation

```{python}
scatter_fit(m, "mean_distance_km", "place_seg_S_a", "Mean home-to-site distance (km)",
            "Place-level segregation $S_a$", "Sa_vs_distance",
            xerr="distance_sem_km", statloc="upper right")
barh_sites(m.set_index("utopia_name")["place_seg_S_a"], "Place-level segregation $S_a$",
           "Sa_per_utopia", fmt="{:.2f}")
```

## The composite and the typology

```{python}
barh_sites(m.set_index("utopia_name")["n_unique_visitors"],
           "Unique visitors (log scale)", "visitors_per_utopia", logx=True)
scatter_fit(m, "composite_score", "n_unique_visitors", "Composite accessibility score",
            "Unique visitors", "composite_vs_visitors", logy=True)
typology_plot(m, "access_typology")
```

Transit and distinctiveness are the two levers a planner controls directly.
Crossing them splits the network into flagship destinations, connected
neighborhood hubs, under-connected attractions, and neighborhood spaces.
